reasoning: The finding is a false positive. Two defects in the original analysis: (1) parse_url($url, PHP_URL_PATH) at line 360 extracts ONLY the path component from the constructed URL — even if HTTP_HOST is attacker-controlled (e.g., 'evil.com'), the call parse_url('//evil.com/wp-admin/...', PHP_URL_PATH) returns '/wp-admin/...' discarding the host entirely. The tainted $host value never survives into the return value of current_page_url(). (2) Every call site in admin/class-favicon-by-realfavicongenerator-admin.php (lines 540, 544, 562, 566) wraps the result in esc_html() before echoing into an href attribute, and the admin notices are only rendered to authenticated administrators. The open-redirect and cache-poisoning scenarios require the attacker-controlled host to propagate into the returned URL, which parse_url prevents. The correct escaping function for href is esc_url() rather than esc_html() (a WPCS coding-standards issue) but this is not exploitable because the value is always a relative path starting with '/'.
